text: The video shows the surgeon addressing a large left indirect inguinal hernia, which extends to the deep internal ring and contains a portion of the sigmoid colon. Adhesions are being carefully taken down, and the hernia sac is manipulated without reducing the cone. The surgeon works across the midline in the preperitoneal plane, revealing a large right direct inguinal hernia and a small right indirect hernia, forming a pantaloon hernia on the right side. The medial umbilical ligaments are encased in fat, and the right direct inguinal hernia extends into the suprapubic space.

The surgeon navigates through the fatty pelvis, pulling down to open the tissue planes, which are encased in fat. The dissection progresses to the preperitoneal plane, moving across the top of the deeper internal ring towards the iliopubic tract. The supravesicular space is taken down, revealing the right direct inguinal hernia. The surgeon uses cautery to push away the anti-sac, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the fatty tissue surrounding a suprapubic hernia extending into the femoral space. Using electrocautery, they carefully dissect along the hernia sac, avoiding the spermatic cord vessels. The hernia sac is visible to the left, with the vas deferens identified on the right.

The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral flap to address a sizable indirect hernia, differentiating the cord lipoma from surrounding fat planes. The cord is pulled up, and excess fat is peeled away, avoiding retroperitoneal fat and nerves near the deep internal ring. The indirect hernia is swept down after medial dissection, with caution taken to avoid thermal transmission to the underlying colon.
The surgeon works laterally, carefully avoiding nerves while pushing fat laterally. The colon begins to drape down into the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the hernia sac, avoiding the cord lipoma to protect the colon. Gentle cautery is applied to the fat side of the contents, ensuring the colon is positioned safely. The surgeon identifies cord structures and maintains a high orientation, allowing the fatty spermatic cord to sweep down. A large vessel is visible, highlighting the vascular nature of the lipomas. The surgeon returns focus to the hernia sac.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side, with fatty cord structures visible below. A large lipoma is peeled back from the cord structures, revealing attenuated cord structures. The hernia sac is visible laterally, while more cord structures are seen medially. The surgeon reorients the structures, pulling the cord structures up and the flap down, while nerves begin to come into view.

The surgeon uses two extra-large barred MaxMesh implants, overlapping them in the middle and securing them to prevent movement. The medial umbilical ligament and surrounding fatty structures are reoriented during closure. The cord lipoma and hernia sac are fixated onto the flap to prevent recurrence, ensuring the mesh remains in place behind the pocket.
